blocks.content: The film was called Millhaven. He shot it in two trips — January, when the town was grey and stripped, and June, when everything was loud and green.
He called her every evening from Georgia.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I filmed the water tower today.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Like a water tower. But his water tower. You'll know what I mean when you see the cut.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I filmed your mom's kitchen. She kept moving around in it like it was nothing.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Did she know you were filming.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I think she knew. She just didn't stop.
He sent her the day's footage every evening. She would watch it, then write.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Read me what you have for the kitchen.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Some people leave. Some people stay and hold the shape of the ones who didn't. They don't call it anything — they just keep the lights on, keep the pantry stocked, keep the door unlocked in case something comes back that never does. They call that love. They call it Tuesday. They call it not thinking about it too hard.
He didn't say anything for a moment.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: That's the whole film. That's everything, right there.
The line stayed open. She didn't try to fill it.
